1: Oracle formula without date — the undated position suggests this oracle extends the Egypt judgment section as a poetic complement to the dated prose oracles.
2: heylilu al-hayyom ('wail! alas for the day!'): the opening lament-cry — heylel/yalal (H3213, wail/howl) — onomatopoeic mourning. hayyom ('the day') — the ominous definite article: a specific day of reckoning is coming, not a general future.
3: ki qarov yom veqarov yom laYHWH ('for the day is near — the day of the LORD is near'): the only explicit yom YHWH in the Ezekiel OAN section. The Day of the LORD against Egypt — the concept developed in Amos 5:18-20 (for Israel); Isa 13 (against Babylon); Joel; now applied to Egypt. et goyim ('time/appointed time of the nations') — the Day is not Israel-specific but universal: it applies to all nations that have set themselves against YHWH's purposes.
4: ubaa cherev bemitzraim ('the sword will come against Egypt'): the Babylonian sword as divine instrument. chavlah cush ('anguish will grip Ethiopia'): Egypt's southern ally suffers collateral damage — the 'anguish' (chil, H2427, writhing pain/anguish, as in labor pangs) is the shock of seeing the dominant regional power fall.
5: The coalition list: Cush, Put, Lud, mixed peoples (erev, H6153/6154), Cub — the nations allied with or dependent on Egypt. erev ('mixed peoples') may be the same as 'mixed multitude' (Exodus 12:38 — non-Israelites who joined the Exodus). The Egyptian coalition represents the multiplicity of nations that have organized their security around Egyptian power rather than YHWH.
6: ga'on uzza tarda ('her proud strength will collapse'): gaon (H1347, pride/majesty/excellence) applied to Egypt's power — the same word Ezekiel uses for Israel's pride (Ezekiel 7:24) and Assyria's (Ezekiel 32:12). ga'on is what YHWH consistently opposes: 'every proud one is an abomination to YHWH' (Proverbs 16:5). Egypt's gaon — built on the claim to divine self-creation of the Nile — is precisely what the judgment dismantles.
7: Desolation formula — the same geographical sweep as Ezekiel 29:10 (Migdol to Syene). The repetition across multiple oracles reinforces the comprehensiveness of the judgment and establishes the established fact through multiple witnesses (Deuteronomy 19:15: a matter is established by two or three witnesses).
8: veyadefu ki ani YHWH beshtoni esh bemitzraim ('they will know that I am the LORD when I set fire to Egypt'): the recognition formula again — but for Egypt's helpers, not only Egypt. The circle of YHWH's self-revelation through judgment extends to all who supported Egypt, including those nations who thought their security lay in Egyptian alliance.
9: biqqayot yetzeyu miti lepahad kush batach ('messengers will go out by ship to terrify complacent Cush'): batach (H982, secure/complacent/at ease) — Ethiopia's false security. The same root as mivtach (Ezekiel 30:16 in Ezekiel 29): trust/security placed in human power rather than YHWH. The fast-moving messengers by boat (along the Nile) carrying news of Egypt's fall: the speed of news transmission in antiquity was along waterways.
10: vehishbati et-hamon mitzraim beyad nebuchadretzar ('I will put an end to the multitude of Egypt by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar'): hamon (H1995, multitude/crowd/noise/wealth) — Egypt's vast population and resources. The hamon that makes Egypt impressive is what YHWH will terminate. Nebuchadnezzar is explicitly named as YHWH's instrument — the Babylonian king who acts without knowing that he serves the divine purpose.
11: araritzei goyim ('the most ruthless of nations'): Ezekiel's characteristic description of Babylon (Ezekiel 28:7; Ezekiel 30:11; Ezekiel 31:12; Ezekiel 32:12) — aritz (H6184, ruthless/violent/terrifying). The ruthlessness that is morally problematic in itself is used as YHWH's instrument. Divine sovereignty working through imperfect instruments is a constant in Ezekiel's theology of history.
12: venatatti yeoroth charavah ('I will make the Nile channels run dry'): the drying of the Nile — the reversal of Egypt's foundational water-source, directly answering Pharaoh's claim 'My Nile is mine.' umakarti et-haaretz beyad-reim ('I will sell the land into the hand of evil men'): makar (H4376, sell) — YHWH selling Egypt, suggesting Egypt's own subordinate status to divine sovereignty; ra (H7451, evil/bad) applied to the Babylonian conquerors.
13: vehishbati gillulim ('I will destroy the idols'): gillulim in Memphis (Noph) — the demolition of Egypt's cultic statuary. Memphis was the ancient capital and religious center, home to the Ptah temple and the Apis bull cult. venassi mieretz mitzraim lo yihyeh od ('there will no longer be a prince in the land of Egypt'): the political and cultic structures collapse simultaneously — religious and political power are co-terminal.
14: Egyptian geography: Pathros = Upper Egypt; Zoan = Tanis in the eastern delta (where the Israelites labored, Psalms 78:43); No = Thebes (No-Amon in Nahum 3:8), the great temple city of Amon-Ra. The judgment reaches the entire Nile valley from south to north, targeting each major religious center.
15: veshafachti chamati al-sin ('I will pour out my wrath on Pelusium'): sin/sewen = Pelusium, the northeastern fortress-gateway to Egypt from Sinai — the military key to Egypt's eastern defense. uveratii et-hamon No ('and cut off the multitude of Thebes'): Thebes, the wealthiest city in the ancient world (Homer, Iliad 9.381: 'Egyptian Thebes where the treasure-houses are fullest'), the religious heart of Egypt.
16: The cascade of Egyptian cities — Pelusium, Thebes, Memphis — three strategic centers of power facing destruction simultaneously. The three cities represent Egypt's military gateway (Pelusium), religious heart (Thebes/Amon), and administrative capital (Memphis/Ptah).
17: On = Heliopolis, the center of the Ra/sun cult (Genesis 41:45 — Potiphera, Joseph's father-in-law, was priest of On). Pi-beseth = Bubastis, center of the Bastet (cat-goddess) cult. Two of Egypt's primary religious cities face military destruction — the judgment targets the cultic infrastructure specifically.
18: vetachpanches chasakh hayyom ('at Tahpanhes the day will grow dark'): Tahpanhes = Daphne, the garrison city in the eastern delta where Jeremiah was taken after the assassination of Gedaliah (Jeremiah 43:7-9). beshivri sham et-mattot mitzraim ('when I break the yokes of Egypt there'): matteh (H4294, rod/yoke/tribe) — the breaking of Egypt's dominion-rods, the power that Egypt exercised over neighboring peoples.
19: veasiti shephath bivmitzraim ('thus I will execute judgments in Egypt'): the chapter concludes with the recognition formula — veyadu ki-ani YHWH. The entire oracle is structured as a theodicy: these judgments are not arbitrary displays of power but the execution of covenant justice that will result in the knowledge of YHWH among the nations.
20: New date: eleventh year, first month, seventh day — April 587 BCE, one year after the Ezekiel 29 oracle, still during the Jerusalem siege. The arm-breaking oracle interprets the failure of Pharaoh Hophra's attempted intervention against Babylon (Jeremiah 37:5-8) as YHWH's action.
21: et-zero'a paro ('the arm of Pharaoh king of Egypt'): zero'a (H2220, arm/strength/power) — the arm as the symbol of military power (Psalms 44:3; Ezekiel 77:15; Isaiah 51:5,9). lo-chatash lashum chatvulot ('it has not been bound up to heal it, no bandage applied'): the wound that is not treated — the military debacle of Hophra's failure to relieve Jerusalem. The army that failed to help Israel has no healing; its defeat is permanent.
22: veshivarti et-zero'otav et-hachazaqah veeth-hanishbaret ('I will break his arms, both the strong and already-broken one'): both arms broken — total military incapacitation. The complete disabling of Egyptian military power means no help can come from that quarter. vehipalti et-hacharev miyado ('I will make the sword fall from his hand'): the disarmed Pharaoh, unable even to hold a weapon, much less wield it in Israel's defense.
23: vahapitzoti et-mitzraim bagoyim ('I will scatter the Egyptians among the nations'): the diaspora-formula for Egypt — the same verb (putz, H6327, scatter/disperse) used for Israel's exile. In the divine economy, all nations are subject to the same covenant-logic: pride leads to scattering; humility enables gathering.
24: vehizzaqti et-zero'ot melek bavel ('I will strengthen the arms of the king of Babylon'): the contrast with broken Pharaoh-arms — YHWH breaks Egyptian arms and strengthens Babylonian arms. zero'a melek bavel versus zero'a paro: the same word (zero'a) used for both, with YHWH controlling which is strong and which is broken. venatatif et-charvi beyado ('I will put my sword in his hand'): Nebuchadnezzar wielding YHWH's sword — the pagan king as the divine executor of judgment.
25: veyadu ki-ani YHWH benitni charevi beyad melek bavel ('they will know that I am the LORD when I put my sword in the hand of the king of Babylon'): the recognition formula is attached to the action through a pagan king — YHWH's self-disclosure happens through the unexpected agent. The Babylonian king is the instrument; YHWH is the sovereign. This is the theological key to understanding geopolitical history in Ezekiel.
26: The concluding formula of the arm-oracle restates the scattering of Egypt (vahapitzoti et-mitzraim) and the recognition formula (veyadu ki-ani YHWH). The chapter ends as it began: YHWH is the subject; Egypt's humiliation is the means of universal knowledge of the LORD.
